summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack’s promise of modular, user-driven tools has been undermined by the absence of a true platform—there’s “no potato” to hold the pieces together. Microsoft Fabric responds by collapsing the stack into a single integrated product, which could appeal to enterprises and shift the market from “legacy vs. modern” to “all‑in‑one vs. best‑of‑breed.” He concludes that the real opportunity is a neutral platform layer that enables genuine modularity, ideally from a cloud provider building infrastructure rather than a monolithic suite.
money_quotes: But this plan has always had one big problem: There is no potato.
The modern data stack has never had a platform.

Ten years in, this the legacy of the modern data stack: Dozens of creative and delightful features, often buried inside of overlapping and overweight products, all clumsily bumping into each other in a soup of startups and data services.
Fabric isn’t a suite of loosely connected services; it’s a single application that has one login experience, one user interface, one storage layer, one permission model, and one monthly bill.
The choice is no longer legacy versus modern, but all-in-one versus best-of-breed.
key_insight: Without a shared platform layer, the modern data stack collapses into bloated, overlapping products—and Fabric’s integrated bet forces the market to choose between seamless consolidation and true best‑of‑breed modularity.
